nativeRange.habitat: Highland streams, hill streams, and slow-moving lowland waters of Java and Sumatra; typically clear, shaded streams with leaf litter, submerged roots, and dense riparian vegetation; rice paddies also reported

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Microsorum, Taxiphyllum, Cryptocoryne, Anubias)
clay pots or PVC piping as cave-style shelters
floating plants (Pistia, Salvinia) to diffuse light

diet.notes: Naturally feeds on aquatic and terrestrial invertebrates. Captive specimens should receive small live or frozen foods regularly — Daphnia, Artemia, chironomid larvae (bloodworm), and micro-worms are all accepted. Dried micro-pellets and flake are taken once recognised as food but should not form the sole diet. The species is prone to obesity, so small amounts fed once or twice daily is preferable to generous portions.

goodWith: small peaceful cyprinids from similar habitats (e.g., Rasbora, Boraras)
small non-fin-nipping tetras in appropriately soft water
other small peaceful wild bettas from compatible water requirements
avoidWith: larger or aggressive fish that will intimidate or harass
boisterous or fast-moving tankmates that stress cryptic species
other male Betta picta without sufficient space and sight-breaks

summary: The Spotted Betta is among the smallest wild bettas in the hobby, reaching around 5 cm. Native to highland streams in Java and Sumatra, it is regarded as one of the hardier wild Betta species, tolerating a wider range of water parameters than most, and is a paternal mouthbrooder that spawns readily in captivity.
careNotes:
The Spotted Betta (Betta picta) is the name-giving species of the picta group — a cluster of small, paternal mouthbrooding wild bettas native to Java and Sumatra, Indonesia. At around 5 cm it is among the smallest members of the genus, and is widely regarded as the hardiest wild Betta, tolerating a wider temperature and pH band than most others in the group. The species is listed as Near Threatened by the IUCN, primarily due to habitat loss.

A 60 × 30 cm tank (approximately 54 litres) is adequate for a pair or small species-only group. The Spotted Betta is cryptic and shy, and a well-planted setup with driftwood, leaf litter, clay-pot caves, and floating plants is essential to encourage natural behaviour. Lighting should be kept subdued. A gentle sponge filter is recommended — the species comes from sluggish waters and strong flow will stress it. A tight-fitting lid is mandatory; like all bettas it is an adept jumper and also needs access to humid air above the water surface for its labyrinth organ (a specialised structure that allows it to breathe atmospheric air directly).

Soft, slightly acidic water is preferred, but the Spotted Betta is more forgiving than many wild bettas in this respect — a pH of 5.5 to 7.5 and a general hardness of 1 to 12 dGH are generally workable, making it more accommodating in tap-water areas. Temperature can range from 22 to 28°C, with the cooler end of that range reflecting its highland stream origins. The species is a facultative air-breather, meaning it can supplement gill respiration by gulping air at the surface.

The intermediate care rating reflects its water-quality sensitivity, cryptic feeding behaviour, and the specialised setup it requires — species-only or very carefully chosen tankmates, dense decor, and a secure lid — rather than any inherent difficulty in keeping it. It is not well suited to a typical community tank. Male-to-male aggression tends to be moderate; pairs or small groups can generally be kept together provided there are ample sight-breaks.
breedingNotes: The Spotted Betta is a paternal mouthbrooder and one of the smallest mouthbrooding bettas in the hobby. Spawning is readily triggered in captivity, often by a water change. After an embracing spawn, the male collects and incubates the fertilised eggs in his buccal cavity (the space inside his mouth) for approximately 9 to 12 days until the fry are free-swimming and fully formed. The male stops feeding during this period and should not be disturbed. The female should be separated after spawning, or the tank should provide adequate hiding so she is not harassed. A brood typically numbers 10 to 40 fry, which are large enough to accept Artemia nauplii (newly hatched brine shrimp) and micro-worm immediately upon release. In the wild the species tends to spawn in mass events, with multiple pairs releasing fry simultaneously — an adaptation thought to reduce individual predation risk. In captivity, single-pair spawns are the norm. A tight-fitting lid and access to humid air above the surface are important for fry as they develop their labyrinth organs.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Betta picta
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided tank dimensions (60×30 cm), water parameters (22–28°C, pH 5.5–7.5, hardness 18–90 ppm), adult size (40–50 mm), diet (invertebrates; live/frozen preferred), breeding (paternal mouthbrooder, 9–12 day incubation), decor preferences, native range (western/central Java), and compatibility guidance (species-only or very peaceful companions).

additional.name: FishBase — Betta picta
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 7.2 cm SL, used 5.5 cm as practical aquarium adult size), temperature 22–24°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness upper limit ~22 dH, family Osphronemidae, order Anabantiformes, native to Indonesia. Notes carnivore trophic level 3.3, facultative air-breathing, IUCN Near Threatened.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Spotted betta
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Valenciennes, 1846), native range (highland streams of Java, Sumatra), adult size ~6 cm, paternal mouthbrooding behaviour, mass-spawning adaptation, IUCN Near Threatened status, common name "Java fighting fish".

additional.name: AKA Wild Betta Blog — Betta picta: The Forgotten Fighter of Java’s Highlands
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Confirmed temperature 23–26°C, pH 6.0–6.5, GH ~10°, adult size up to 6.5 cm TL, paternal mouthbrooding, specific Java localities (Mojokerto, Madura, Candi, Madiun, Ambarawa) and western Sumatra (Lake Singkarak area). Notes dense vegetation and submerged roots as preferred decor.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Species groups within the genus Betta
additional.notes: Confirmed Betta picta as the type/name-giving species of the picta group (small paternal mouthbrooding wild bettas); group distinguished by rounded unpaired fins, dark distal fin margins, pre- and post-orbital stripes, and iridescent opercle scales.

fish.breedingNotes: The Spotted Betta is a paternal mouthbrooder, and spawning is readily triggered in captivity, often by a water change. After the spawning embrace, the male collects the fertilised eggs and incubates them in his mouth for approximately 9 to 12 days until the fry are fully formed and free-swimming. The male stops feeding during this period and should not be disturbed. The female should be separated or given adequate hiding space after spawning, as she may be harassed. A typical brood numbers 10 to 40 fry, which are large enough to accept Artemia nauplii (newly hatched brine shrimp) immediately upon release.
